a: Israel prepares to enter Canaan. The LORD commissions Joshua and commands courage and meditation on the law (1); Rahab hides the spies at Jericho (2); the nation crosses the Jordan on dry ground as the ark goes ahead, and twelve memorial stones are set up (3–4); and the new generation is circumcised, keeps the Passover, the manna ceases, and the commander of the LORD's army appears to Joshua (5).
summary: Joshua commissioned (1), Rahab and the spies (2), the Jordan crossed with memorial stones (3–4), and circumcision, Passover, and the LORD's commander (5).

a: Israel conquers the land. Jericho's walls fall after the seven-day march (6); Achan's sin brings defeat at Ai until it is judged, then Ai is taken (7–8); the Gibeonites deceive Israel into a treaty (9); the southern coalition is routed when the sun stands still (10); and the northern kings under Jabin of Hazor are defeated (11). Chapter 12 lists the thirty-one kings Israel struck down.
summary: Jericho falls (6), Achan's sin and Ai (7–8), the Gibeonite deception (9), and the southern and northern campaigns with the defeated kings listed (10–12).

a: The land is divided by lot among the tribes. Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh receive land east of the Jordan; Caleb claims Hebron, and Judah, Ephraim, and the remaining tribes receive their allotments through the lot at Shiloh (13–19). Cities of refuge are appointed for the manslayer (20), and forty-eight Levitical cities are assigned to the priestly tribe (21).
summary: The tribal inheritances are distributed by lot, Caleb takes Hebron, and the cities of refuge and Levitical cities are appointed (13–21).

a: The eastern tribes return home and build an altar by the Jordan, nearly provoking civil war until they explain it is a witness, not for sacrifice (22). Joshua delivers a farewell charge urging Israel to cleave to the LORD (23). At Shechem he rehearses the LORD's mighty acts and leads a covenant renewal, declaring "as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD"; Joshua and Eleazar then die (24).
summary: The altar of witness (22), Joshua's farewell charge (23), and the covenant renewal at Shechem before his death (24).

a: After Joshua's death the tribes fail to drive out the Canaanites, and a new generation forsakes the LORD, beginning the cycle of sin, oppression, crying out, and deliverance (1–2). God raises judges: Othniel against Cushan-rishathaim, Ehud who kills Eglon of Moab, and Shamgar (3); then Deborah and Barak defeat Sisera, whom Jael kills with a tent peg, celebrated in the Song of Deborah (4–5).
summary: Incomplete conquest and the cycle of apostasy (1–2), the judges Othniel, Ehud, and Shamgar (3), and Deborah, Barak, and Jael against Sisera (4–5).

a: Gideon is called to deliver Israel from Midian; he tears down Baal's altar, tests God with the fleece, and routs the host with three hundred men and trumpets (6–8). Abimelech seizes power at Shechem and dies when a millstone is dropped on him (9). Lesser judges Tola and Jair serve (10), and Jephthah defeats Ammon after his rash vow concerning his daughter, followed by the Shibboleth conflict with Ephraim (11–12).
summary: Gideon defeats Midian (6–8), Abimelech's bloody reign (9), and Jephthah's victory over Ammon, his rash vow, and the Ephraimite clash (10–12).

a: Samson, a Nazirite from birth, is given to fight the Philistines; he marries, kills a lion, slays a thousand with a donkey's jawbone, is betrayed by Delilah, and destroys the Dagon temple in his death (13–16). The closing chapters depict Israel's deep corruption: Micah's idol and the Danite migration (17–18), and the Levite's concubine, the war against Benjamin, and the wives for the survivors (19–21), with the refrain that everyone did what was right in his own eyes.
summary: Samson's career against the Philistines (13–16) and the depravity of Micah's idol, the Danites, and the Benjamite war (17–21).

a: Hannah prays for a son and dedicates Samuel to the LORD, singing in thanksgiving (1–2). Eli's wicked sons are condemned, and the LORD calls the boy Samuel as prophet (3). The ark is captured by the Philistines, Eli dies, but the ark afflicts Dagon and the cities until it is returned (4–6). Samuel leads Israel to repentance at Mizpah, and the LORD routs the Philistines, commemorated by the Ebenezer stone (7).
summary: Samuel's birth and Hannah's song (1–2), his prophetic call (3), the ark captured and returned (4–6), and victory at Mizpah (7).

a: Israel demands a king, and Samuel warns of the king's ways (8). Saul is anointed and confirmed king, then leads Israel against the Ammonites (9–11). Samuel gives his farewell address (12). Saul is rejected from a lasting dynasty for offering sacrifice unlawfully at Gilgal (13), Jonathan's bold faith brings victory over the Philistines (14), and Saul is finally rejected as king for sparing Agag and the spoil of Amalek (15).
summary: Israel demands a king and Saul is anointed and reigns (8–11), Samuel's farewell (12), and Saul's twofold rejection for disobedience (13–15).

a: Samuel secretly anoints David, and David enters Saul's court as a musician (16). David kills Goliath in the Valley of Elah (17), and his rising fame stirs Saul's jealousy despite Jonathan's covenant friendship and Michal's marriage to David (18–19). Saul's hostility forces David to flee; Jonathan warns him, the priests of Nob are massacred, and David escapes repeatedly in the wilderness, including at Keilah and Ziph (20–23).
summary: David anointed (16), Goliath slain (17), Saul's jealousy and Jonathan's friendship (18–20), and David's flight and Saul's pursuit (21–23).

a: David twice spares Saul's life, in the cave and in the camp, refusing to harm the LORD's anointed (24, 26). Samuel dies, and the Nabal-Abigail episode unfolds (25). David takes refuge among the Philistines at Ziklag (27). Saul, abandoned by God, consults the medium at Endor (28). David is dismissed from the Philistine battle line and recovers Ziklag from Amalekite raiders (29–30). On Mount Gilboa Saul and his sons, including Jonathan, fall, and Saul takes his own life (31).
summary: David spares Saul twice (24, 26), Nabal and Abigail (25), the medium at Endor (28), the Ziklag rescue (30), and Saul's death at Gilboa (31).

a: David laments Saul and Jonathan, then is anointed king over Judah at Hebron while Ish-bosheth rules the north, leading to civil war until Abner and Ish-bosheth are murdered (1–4). David is anointed king over all Israel, captures Jerusalem, and defeats the Philistines (5). He brings the ark to Jerusalem, and Uzzah dies for touching it (6). God makes the Davidic covenant, promising David an everlasting throne (7), and David's victories expand the kingdom (8).
summary: David's lament and reign at Hebron (1–4), Jerusalem captured and the ark brought up (5–6), the Davidic covenant (7), and David's conquests (8).

a: David shows kindness to Mephibosheth for Jonathan's sake (9). War with the Ammonites and Arameans frames David's great sin: his adultery with Bathsheba and the murder of Uriah (10–11). Nathan confronts David, who repents, yet the child dies though Solomon is later born (12). The sword does not leave David's house: Amnon rapes Tamar, Absalom kills Amnon and flees, and Joab arranges his partial return (13–14).
summary: Kindness to Mephibosheth (9), David's sin with Bathsheba and Nathan's rebuke (10–12), and the troubles of Amnon, Tamar, and Absalom (13–14).

a: Absalom conspires and seizes the throne, forcing David to flee Jerusalem; Absalom is defeated and killed by Joab, and David mourns him bitterly (15–18). David returns amid tension between Judah and Israel, and Sheba's revolt is crushed (19–20). The appendix records the Gibeonite famine, wars with Philistine giants, David's song and last words, his mighty men (21–23), and the census that brings plague, ended at Araunah's threshing floor where David builds an altar (24).
summary: Absalom's revolt and death (15–18), David's return and Sheba's revolt (19–20), and the closing appendix of famine, songs, heroes, and the census (21–24).

a: As David nears death, Adonijah's bid for the throne fails and Solomon is made king; David charges him before dying (1–2). Solomon asks for wisdom, displays it in judgment, and organizes his kingdom (3–4). He builds the temple and his palace, and dedicates the temple with prayer as the glory fills it (5–8). God appears again with covenant promises and warnings (9). The queen of Sheba visits in Solomon's splendor (10), but his foreign wives turn his heart to idols, so God announces the kingdom's division (11).
summary: Solomon secured as king (1–2), his wisdom and temple-building (3–8), his glory (9–10), and his apostasy bringing the threat of division (11).

a: After Solomon's death Rehoboam's harshness splits the kingdom; Jeroboam leads the northern ten tribes and sets up golden calves at Bethel and Dan (12–13). Both kingdoms decline through the reigns of Jeroboam, Rehoboam, Abijah, and Asa in the south, and a violent succession in the north through Nadab, Baasha, Elah, Zimri, and Omri, until Ahab takes the throne and does more evil than all before him (14–16).
summary: The kingdom divides under Rehoboam and Jeroboam with the golden calves (12–13), followed by the early kings of Judah and Israel down to Ahab (14–16).

a: Elijah confronts Ahab and Jezebel's Baal worship: he is fed by ravens and the widow of Zarephath, then defeats the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel by calling down fire (17–18). Fleeing Jezebel, he meets God at Horeb and calls Elisha (19). Ahab defeats Aram but spares Ben-hadad (20), seizes Naboth's vineyard and is condemned (21), and dies in battle at Ramoth-gilead as Micaiah had foretold (22).
summary: Elijah's ministry against Baal and the Carmel contest (17–18), his flight to Horeb and Elisha's call (19), and Ahab's wars, Naboth's vineyard, and death (20–22).

a: Elijah calls down fire on Ahaziah's messengers, then is taken up in a whirlwind as Elisha receives a double portion of his spirit (1–2). Elisha performs many miracles: the widow's oil, the Shunammite's son raised, the deadly stew healed, Naaman cleansed of leprosy, and the floating axe head (3–6). The Aramean siege of Samaria is lifted as the lepers find the abandoned camp (7), and Elisha's words touch Hazael's rise and Judah's kings (8).
summary: Elijah taken up and Elisha empowered (1–2), Elisha's miracles including Naaman's cleansing (3–6), and the relief of Samaria (7–8).

a: Jehu is anointed and purges the house of Ahab, killing Joram, Jezebel, and the Baal worshipers, yet keeps the golden calves (9–10). In Judah, Athaliah usurps the throne until the priest Jehoiada crowns young Joash and restores temple worship (11–12). The narrative tracks the declining kings of both realms through Jehoahaz, Jehoash, Jeroboam II, and others (13–15), until Assyria conquers Samaria and exiles the northern kingdom for its idolatry (16–17).
summary: Jehu's purge of Ahab's house (9–10), Athaliah and Joash in Judah (11–12), the later kings, and the fall of Samaria to Assyria (13–17).

a: Hezekiah reforms Judah and is delivered from Sennacherib's Assyrian siege through Isaiah's word, though his life is extended after illness (18–20). Manasseh's long, idolatrous reign provokes the LORD, and Amon follows (21). Josiah finds the Book of the Law, leads sweeping reforms and a great Passover, but dies at Megiddo (22–23). Under his weak successors Babylon under Nebuchadnezzar besieges Jerusalem, destroys the temple, and carries Judah into exile (24–25).
summary: Hezekiah's deliverance (18–20), Manasseh's evil (21), Josiah's reforms (22–23), and Jerusalem's fall and the Babylonian exile (24–25).

a: The book opens with extended genealogies tracing God's people from Adam through the patriarchs and the twelve tribes of Israel (1–7). Special attention falls on Judah and the line of David, on Levi and the priests, and on Benjamin and the family of Saul (8). The section closes with the genealogies of those who returned from exile to resettle Jerusalem, including priests, Levites, and gatekeepers, anchoring the restored community in its heritage (9).
summary: Genealogies from Adam through the tribes of Israel, highlighting Judah, Levi, and Benjamin, and the post-exilic resettlers of Jerusalem (1–9).

a: After Saul's death on Gilboa, David is made king over all Israel and captures Jerusalem with his mighty men (10–12). David brings the ark to Jerusalem, with Uzzah's death and then proper Levitical transport, and appoints singers (13–16). God establishes the Davidic covenant, promising an enduring throne, and David responds in prayer (17). David then wins victories over the Philistines, Moab, Ammon, and the Arameans, extending his kingdom (18–19).
summary: Saul's death and David made king (10–12), the ark brought to Jerusalem (13–16), the Davidic covenant (17), and David's conquests (18–19).

a: David's wars conclude with victories over the Philistine giants (20). His census brings a plague, halted at Ornan's threshing floor, the future temple site (21). David then makes extensive preparations for the temple Solomon will build, organizing the Levites, priestly divisions, musicians, gatekeepers, and military and civil officers (22–27). In a final assembly David charges Solomon and the people, gives generously for the temple, blesses the LORD, and dies after Solomon is made king (28–29).
summary: David's last wars and census (20–21), his temple preparations and organization of the Levites (22–27), and his charge to Solomon before his death (28–29).

a: Solomon asks God for wisdom and is granted wisdom and wealth (1). He builds the temple in Jerusalem with materials from Hiram and dedicates it; the glory of the LORD fills the house and fire consumes the sacrifices at his prayer (2–7). God appears, promising blessing if the people humble themselves and pray (7). Solomon's building projects, wealth, and wisdom culminate in the visit of the queen of Sheba, and he dies in great splendor (8–9).
summary: Solomon's wisdom (1), the building and dedication of the temple with the glory filling it (2–7), and his wealth, the queen of Sheba, and his death (8–9).

a: Rehoboam's harshness splits the kingdom, and the Chronicler follows the kings of Judah (10–12). Abijah defeats Jeroboam by trusting the LORD (13). Asa institutes reforms and wins against the Ethiopians but later relies on Aram rather than God (14–16). Jehoshaphat strengthens Judah, sends teachers of the law, appoints judges, and sees a great deliverance from Moab and Ammon when the people sing and the LORD ambushes the invaders (17–20).
summary: The division under Rehoboam (10–12), Abijah's and Asa's reigns (13–16), and Jehoshaphat's reforms and miraculous victory (17–20).

a: Judah declines under wicked kings: Jehoram and Ahaziah, then Athaliah's usurpation until the priest Jehoiada crowns young Joash (21–23). Joash repairs the temple but turns away after Jehoiada dies and murders Zechariah (24). Amaziah, Uzziah (struck with leprosy for presuming on the priesthood), and Jotham follow with mixed faithfulness (25–27), and Ahaz plunges into deep idolatry, suffering defeat and shutting the temple (28).
summary: The wicked kings, Athaliah, and Joash's reign and fall (21–24), then Amaziah, Uzziah, Jotham, and the apostate Ahaz (25–28).

a: Hezekiah reopens and cleanses the temple, restores worship, keeps a great Passover, and is delivered from Sennacherib (29–32). Manasseh's evil reign ends in repentance after Babylonian captivity, and Amon follows (33). Josiah finds the Book of the Law, leads sweeping reforms and a great Passover, but dies at Megiddo (34–35). Under his successors Judah's sin ripens until Babylon destroys Jerusalem and the temple and carries the people into exile, closing with Cyrus's decree to return (36).
summary: Hezekiah's reforms and deliverance (29–32), Manasseh and Josiah (33–35), and Jerusalem's fall, the exile, and Cyrus's decree (36).

a: Cyrus of Persia decrees that the Jews may return and rebuild the temple (1). A large company returns under Zerubbabel and Jeshua, listed by family (2). They rebuild the altar, resume sacrifices, and lay the temple's foundation amid weeping and joy (3). Opposition from the surrounding peoples halts the work until the prophets Haggai and Zechariah stir the people, and the temple is finished and dedicated under Darius, followed by a joyful Passover (4–6).
summary: Cyrus's decree and the return under Zerubbabel (1–2), the altar and foundation laid (3), and the temple completed despite opposition (4–6).

a: Ezra the scribe, skilled in the law of Moses, travels from Babylon to Jerusalem with the favor of King Artaxerxes and a commission to teach God's law (7). He gathers the returning company, proclaims a fast for protection, and brings the temple offerings safely (8). Learning that many, including leaders, have intermarried with the surrounding peoples, Ezra mourns and confesses the sin (9), and the people covenant to put away the foreign wives in repentance (10).
summary: Ezra's journey and commission from Artaxerxes (7–8) and his response to the crisis of intermarriage with confession and reform (9–10).

a: Hearing of Jerusalem's broken walls, Nehemiah, cupbearer to Artaxerxes, prays and gains permission to return (1–2). He inspects the ruins and organizes the people to rebuild the wall, each working on a section (2–3). Despite mockery and threats from Sanballat, Tobiah, and Geshem, the workers build with a weapon at hand, and Nehemiah addresses internal economic injustice (4–5). The wall is completed in fifty-two days, and the genealogy of the returnees is recorded (6–7).
summary: Nehemiah's prayer and return (1–2), the wall rebuilt amid opposition and economic reform (3–5), and its completion in fifty-two days (6–7).

a: Ezra reads the Law to the assembled people, who weep and then rejoice, keeping the Feast of Booths (8). The people confess their sins in a great prayer rehearsing God's faithfulness and bind themselves by a written covenant (9–10). Jerusalem is repopulated by lot, leaders are listed, and the wall is dedicated with joyful processions and music (11–12). Nehemiah returns later to correct abuses concerning Tobiah, the Levites' support, Sabbath-breaking, and mixed marriages (13).
summary: The reading of the Law and Feast of Booths (8), the covenant of confession (9–10), the dedication of the wall (11–12), and Nehemiah's final reforms (13).

a: In Susa, King Ahasuerus deposes Queen Vashti, and the Jewish exile Esther, raised by her cousin Mordecai, is chosen as the new queen without revealing her people (1–2). Mordecai uncovers a plot against the king but is later refused honor by the proud Haman, who, enraged that Mordecai will not bow, secures a decree to destroy all the Jews (3). Mordecai urges Esther to intervene "for such a time as this"; she fasts and approaches the king, inviting him and Haman to banquets (4–5).
summary: Esther becomes queen (1–2), Haman plots genocide against the Jews (3), and Esther resolves to intercede and prepares her banquets (4–5).

a: The king, sleepless, learns of Mordecai's earlier loyalty and forces Haman to honor him publicly (6). At Esther's second banquet she exposes Haman's plot, and the king has Haman hanged on the gallows he built for Mordecai (7). A counter-decree allows the Jews to defend themselves, and they triumph over their enemies (8–9). The festival of Purim is established to commemorate their deliverance, and Mordecai is exalted to high office under the king (9–10).
summary: Mordecai honored and Haman hanged (6–7), the Jews delivered by counter-decree, and the feast of Purim instituted with Mordecai's promotion (8–10).
